label: Qing Dynasty
pitch:
When Manchus Capture Beijing in 1644, they founded China's last imperial dynasty — and for 150 years, the Kangxi Emperor and Qianlong Emperor presided over the world's largest economy and most populous nation. Then the First Opium War shattered everything, and by the time Puyi Abdicates in 1912 at age six, two thousand years of imperial rule were over.

🔑 Key Facts:
• Lin Zexu Destroys Opium — confiscating 1,000 tons of British opium — triggered the war that began China's 'century of humiliation'
• The Taiping Rebellion was a Christian-inspired civil war that killed 20-30 million people, making it the bloodiest conflict before World War I
• The Siku Quanshu Compiled cataloged all of Chinese knowledge in 36,000 volumes — while also censoring anything anti-Manchu
• Empress Dowager Cixi dominated Qing politics for nearly five decades, crushing the Hundred Days' Reform after just 103 days

🤔 Why It Matters: Modern China's determination to never be humiliated again — driving everything from its economic rise to its military buildup — traces directly to the Qing dynasty's traumatic collapse.

🎲 Wild Card: The Macartney Mission in 1793 saw Britain send its finest diplomat to open trade; Qianlong sent him home with a letter declaring China had no need for European goods — a dismissal that Britain would answer with warships 46 years later.

spans.label: Manchu Conquest
spans.description: Manchu forces seized Beijing and spent four decades subjugating the rest of China, crushing Ming loyalists and imposing the queue hairstyle. The conquest was completed when Kangxi defeated the Zheng dynasty on Taiwan in 1683.

spans.label: High Qing
spans.description: Under Kangxi, Yongzheng, and Qianlong, China reached its greatest territorial extent and became the world's largest economy. This era saw flourishing arts, the compilation of the Siku Quanshu, and expansion into Xinjiang, Tibet, and Central Asia.

spans.label: Qing Decline
spans.description: Internal rebellions like the White Lotus revolt exposed Qing military decay, while the Opium Wars shattered China's sense of invulnerability. The Taiping Rebellion killed tens of millions and left the dynasty permanently weakened.

spans.label: Late Qing
spans.description: Empress Dowager Cixi dominated a dynasty struggling between reform and tradition as foreign powers carved out spheres of influence. The Boxer Rebellion, defeat by Japan, and the Xinhai Revolution ended two thousand years of imperial rule.

events.label: Nurhaci Founds the Later Jin
events.description: The Jurchen chieftain Nurhaci unified the scattered tribes of Manchuria and proclaimed the Later Jin state, declaring himself khan and openly challenging Ming overlordship.

events.label: Banner System Established
events.description: Nurhaci organized Manchu society into Eight Banners — military-civilian units identified by colored flags — that became the hereditary backbone of Qing power and garrisoned cities across China.

events.label: Nurhaci's Seven Grievances
events.description: Nurhaci issued a manifesto of seven grievances against the Ming and launched open war, beginning the long Manchu campaign to break Ming control of the northeast.

events.label: The Manchu Name Adopted
events.description: Hong Taiji formally renamed the Jurchen people 'Manchu,' forging a new ethnic identity distinct from the Ming Chinese they intended to rule.

events.label: Hong Taiji Proclaims the Qing
events.description: Nurhaci's son Hong Taiji renamed the dynasty 'Qing' (Pure), claimed the Mandate of Heaven, and built the bureaucratic state that would conquer China.

events.label: Dorgon's Regency
events.description: Prince Dorgon ruled as regent for the child Shunzhi Emperor, directing the conquest of China, the move to Beijing, and the harsh policies that secured Manchu rule.

events.label: Shunzhi Emperor
events.description: The first Qing emperor to rule from Beijing consolidated Manchu control and began integrating Chinese administrative systems while Dorgon held real power.

events.label: Wu Sangui Opens Shanhai Pass
events.description: Ming general Wu Sangui, trapped between rebel Li Zicheng and the Manchu army, opened the Great Wall's Shanhai Pass and let the Qing forces through toward Beijing.

events.label: Manchus Capture Beijing
events.description: Manchu forces entered Beijing after the Ming dynasty collapsed, establishing the Qing as China's last imperial dynasty.

events.label: Queue Order Enforced
events.description: The Manchu government forced all Chinese men to adopt the Manchu shaved-front queue hairstyle on pain of death, provoking widespread resistance and massacres.

events.label: Yangzhou Massacre
events.description: After the city resisted the queue order and the conquest, Qing armies slaughtered the population of Yangzhou over ten days, a terror that broke southern resistance.

events.label: Southern Ming Resistance
events.description: Ming princes set up rival courts across the south, fielding loyalist armies that the Qing spent nearly two decades hunting down before the last claimant was executed.

events.label: Koxinga's Sea Kingdom
events.description: The Ming loyalist warlord Koxinga drove the Dutch from Taiwan and built an offshore resistance state that defied the Qing until 1683.

events.label: The Manchu-Han Dyarchy
events.description: The Qing ran a dual government in which Manchu and Han officials staffed paired posts, a power-sharing fiction that kept a tiny conquest elite atop a vast Chinese bureaucracy.

events.label: Kangxi Emperor
events.description: One of China's greatest rulers reigned for 61 years, suppressing revolts, conquering Taiwan, and patronizing arts and scholarship.

events.label: The Oboi Regency
events.description: Four Manchu regents, dominated by the strongman Oboi, ruled for the boy Kangxi until the teenage emperor staged a daring coup and arrested Oboi in person.

events.label: Revolt of the Three Feudatories
events.description: Three powerful Chinese generals, led by Wu Sangui, rebelled against Kangxi's attempt to abolish their semi-autonomous southern domains, threatening to split the empire.

events.label: Qing Conquers Taiwan
events.description: Kangxi's fleet crushed the Zheng dynasty loyalists at Penghu, incorporating Taiwan into the Chinese empire for the first time and ending the last conquest-era resistance.

events.label: Treaty of Nerchinsk
events.description: China's first formal treaty with a European power fixed the Russo-Chinese border in the Amur region and regulated frontier trade, negotiated with Jesuit interpreters.

events.label: Kangxi's Dzungar Wars
events.description: Kangxi personally led armies onto the steppe against the Dzungar Mongol leader Galdan, beginning a 70-year struggle for Inner Asia.

events.label: Jesuits at the Qing Court
events.description: Jesuit missionaries served as astronomers, mapmakers, and advisors to Kangxi, bridging European and Chinese scientific knowledge at the imperial court.

events.label: Chinese Rites Controversy
events.description: The Pope banned Chinese Christians from honoring Confucius and ancestors, leading an offended Kangxi to expel uncooperative missionaries and curb Christianity.

events.label: Kangxi Dictionary
events.description: The most comprehensive Chinese character dictionary was completed, containing over 47,000 characters and remaining the standard reference for centuries.

events.label: The Sacred Edict
events.description: Kangxi issued sixteen moral maxims, later expanded and read aloud in villages twice a month, turning Confucian orthodoxy into a tool of mass ideological control.

events.label: Kangxi's Southern Tours
events.description: Kangxi made six grand inspection tours of the wealthy Yangtze south, courting Chinese literati, checking water control, and projecting Manchu legitimacy.

events.label: Pu Songling's Strange Tales
events.description: Pu Songling completed 'Strange Tales from a Chinese Studio,' nearly five hundred supernatural short stories that became a classic of vernacular fiction.

events.label: The Perpetual Tax Freeze
events.description: Kangxi froze the head-tax quota forever, declaring later population growth untaxed — a populist gesture that later starved the state of revenue as numbers exploded.

events.label: Imperial Examinations at Peak
events.description: The civil service examination system reached its fullest development, selecting officials through rigorous testing on the Confucian classics and the rigid eight-legged essay.

events.label: Smallpox Variolation Spreads
events.description: The Manchu court, terrified of smallpox, promoted variolation — deliberate infection with mild material to induce immunity — a Chinese practice that later reached Europe.

events.label: Tibetan Buddhism Patronized
events.description: The Qing emperors embraced Tibetan Buddhism and hosted the Fifth Dalai Lama, using the faith to legitimize their rule over Mongol and Tibetan subjects.

events.label: Yongzheng Emperor
events.description: The industrious emperor reformed taxation, centralized power, and cracked down on corruption, strengthening the empire his father built in just thirteen relentless years.

events.label: The Grand Council Created
events.description: Yongzheng set up a small inner cabinet to run military and state secrets directly under the throne, bypassing the bureaucracy and concentrating power in the emperor's hands.

events.label: The Palace Memorial System
events.description: Yongzheng expanded a system of secret sealed reports sent straight to the emperor, letting him spy on officials and govern through a web of confidential correspondence.

events.label: Yongzheng's Fiscal Reforms
events.description: Yongzheng merged the head tax into the land tax and legalized a 'meltage fee' to fund honest salaries, broadening the base while attacking the roots of corruption.

events.label: Emancipation of the "Mean People"
events.description: Yongzheng abolished the hereditary debased status of several outcast groups, freeing entertainers, beggars, and fishing clans to register as commoners.

events.label: The Secret Succession System
events.description: Scarred by a brutal succession struggle, Yongzheng began sealing the heir's name behind a palace tablet, opened only at death, to end deadly princely feuds.

events.label: The Zeng Jing Affair
events.description: When a minor scholar plotted rebellion on anti-Manchu grounds, Yongzheng spared him and instead published a book-length rebuttal arguing Manchu legitimacy.

events.label: Christianity Banned
events.description: Yongzheng outlawed Christianity as a heterodox sect, expelled missionaries to Macau, and confiscated churches, ending the brief Catholic foothold at court.

events.label: The Qinghai Pacification
events.description: Yongzheng crushed the revolt of the Khoshut Mongol prince Lobzang Danjin, bringing the Tibetan-Mongol borderland of Qinghai firmly under Qing administration.

events.label: Qianlong Emperor
events.description: Qianlong expanded the empire to its greatest extent, conquering Xinjiang and Tibet, while presiding over China's cultural golden age and its slide into complacency.

events.label: Ten Great Campaigns
events.description: Qianlong's series of frontier wars expanded the empire into Central Asia, Tibet, Burma, and Vietnam, projecting power at staggering cost to the treasury.

events.label: The Destruction of the Dzungars
events.description: Qianlong's armies annihilated the Dzungar Mongol confederation, killing or displacing most of its population in what historians describe as a deliberate genocide.

events.label: The Conquest of Xinjiang
events.description: After defeating the Dzungars and the Altishahr Khojas, Qianlong annexed the vast Tarim and Zungar basins as the 'New Frontier' — Xinjiang — doubling the empire's size.

events.label: The Jinchuan Campaigns
events.description: Two ruinously expensive wars against small Tibetan hill chieftaincies in Sichuan exposed how costly and clumsy the celebrated Qing war machine had become.

events.label: The Burma Campaigns
events.description: Four Qing invasions of Burma ended in disease-ravaged failure, a humiliating limit to Qianlong's expansion masked as a tributary victory.

events.label: Siku Quanshu Compiled
events.description: The largest literary compilation in Chinese history was assembled under Qianlong, cataloging the entire body of Chinese knowledge in roughly 36,000 volumes.

events.label: Qing Literary Inquisition
events.description: Alongside the Siku Quanshu, Qianlong ran systematic censorship campaigns, destroying or altering thousands of texts deemed anti-Manchu and persecuting their authors.

events.label: Dream of the Red Chamber
events.description: Cao Xueqin's masterpiece was printed in full, widely regarded as the greatest novel in Chinese literature and an elegiac portrait of a declining aristocratic clan.

events.label: Cao Xueqin
events.description: Born into a once-grand Manchu bannerman family ruined by imperial disfavor, Cao Xueqin drew on its fall to write China's greatest novel.

events.label: Castiglione's Court Painting
events.description: The Jesuit painter Giuseppe Castiglione fused European perspective with Chinese brushwork at Qianlong's court, defining the visual style of the High Qing.

events.label: Imperial Garden Palaces Flourish
events.description: Qianlong poured fortunes into vast garden retreats northwest of Beijing, showcasing imperial landscape architecture at the height of Qing wealth and taste.

events.label: The Chengde Mountain Resort
events.description: The emperors built a sprawling summer capital at Chengde, where they hunted, received Inner Asian leaders, and ruled an empire of Manchus, Mongols, and Tibetans.

events.label: The Putuo Zongcheng Temple
events.description: Qianlong built a half-scale replica of Lhasa's Potala Palace at Chengde, architecture as diplomacy proclaiming the Qing as patrons of Tibetan Buddhism.

events.label: Potala Palace Expanded
events.description: The Qing backed the great expansion of the Potala Palace in Lhasa, reinforcing Tibet's bond to the empire through Buddhist patronage and the resident amban.

events.label: The Rise of Heshen
events.description: A handsome young guardsman became the aging Qianlong's favorite and most powerful minister, building history's largest fortune through systemic corruption.

events.label: Macartney Mission
events.description: Britain's diplomatic mission to open trade was rebuffed by Qianlong, who declared in a famous letter that China possessed all things and had no need of European goods.

events.label: The Golden Urn Edict
events.description: After a war in Tibet, Qianlong imposed the Golden Urn lottery for selecting reincarnated lamas, asserting Qing authority over Tibetan Buddhist succession.

events.label: Peking Opera Emerges
events.description: Anhui opera troupes summoned for Qianlong's birthday stayed in the capital, blending regional styles into the form that became Peking opera.

events.label: Evidential Scholarship Flourishes
events.description: Scholars turned from speculation to rigorous textual evidence, building a Chinese tradition of philological and historical proof — partly a refuge from political danger.

events.label: Canton System Established
events.description: Qianlong restricted all Western trade to the single port of Canton, creating a tightly controlled funnel that channeled enormous wealth but bred foreign resentment.

events.label: The Cohong Merchants
events.description: A licensed guild of Chinese merchants held a monopoly on dealing with foreigners at Canton, growing fabulously rich while answering personally for the foreigners' conduct.

events.label: Tea Trade with Europe Booms
events.description: Chinese tea exports to Europe grew enormously, creating a trade imbalance Britain paid for in silver — and would later try to reverse with opium.

events.label: The Great Silver Inflow
events.description: For over a century, the world's silver poured into China to pay for tea, silk, and porcelain, making the Qing the hub of the early global economy.

events.label: Jingdezhen Porcelain Peak
events.description: Imperial kilns at Jingdezhen reached their artistic zenith under Kangxi and Yongzheng, producing porcelain prized — and imitated — by collectors worldwide.

events.label: The Population Explosion
events.description: Peace, new crops, and the tax freeze tripled China's population from roughly 150 to over 400 million in a century, straining land and seeding future unrest.

events.label: New World Crops Transform Farming
events.description: Maize, sweet potato, and peanut — American crops that grew on poor hill soil — let Qing farmers feed the booming population by colonizing previously useless land.

events.label: The Grain Tribute System
events.description: Millions of tons of rice moved north each year up the Grand Canal to feed Beijing and the banner garrisons, a logistical lifeline whose decay later crippled the state.

events.label: The Salt Monopoly and the Merchant Princes
events.description: State-licensed salt merchants, especially at Yangzhou, amassed legendary fortunes from the salt monopoly, bankrolling gardens, scholarship, and the imperial purse.

events.label: The Overseas Chinese
events.description: Traders and laborers from the crowded southeast coast spread across Southeast Asia, building merchant communities the Qing officially disowned but the world came to rely on.

events.label: The Eccentrics of Yangzhou
events.description: A circle of individualist painters in salt-rich Yangzhou broke court conventions, selling expressive art to merchant patrons in one of China's first true art markets.

events.label: White Lotus Rebellion
events.description: A massive millenarian Buddhist revolt raged across central China for eight years, exposing the rot in Qing armies and finances and ending the High Qing illusion.

events.label: The Fall of Heshen
events.description: Days after Qianlong died, the new Jiaqing Emperor forced Heshen to commit suicide and seized a fortune said to rival years of state revenue.

events.label: The Jiaqing Emperor
events.description: Qianlong's son inherited rebellion, debt, and corruption, and despite earnest frugality could only slow, not reverse, the dynasty's decline.

events.label: The Eight Trigrams Uprising
events.description: Millenarian rebels infiltrated the Forbidden City itself and fought inside the palace walls, a shocking breach that revealed how fragile the dynasty had become.

events.label: The Daoguang Emperor
events.description: A frugal, conscientious, but limited emperor presided over the opium crisis and the First Opium War, watching the old order crack under pressures he could not grasp.

events.label: The Opium Crisis
events.description: British and American traders flooded China with Indian opium, hooking millions of addicts and reversing the silver flow that had underwritten the Qing economy.

events.label: The Silver Drain Reverses
events.description: As opium payments drained silver out of China, its price soared, the copper-silver economy buckled, and peasants' real tax burden quietly doubled.

events.label: The Decay of the Banner Armies
events.description: The once-fearsome Eight Banners had become a pampered, unfit hereditary caste, leaving the dynasty militarily hollow just as crises multiplied.

events.label: The Statecraft Revival
events.description: Reformist scholars like Gong Zizhen revived a 'statecraft' tradition, urging practical solutions to real crises against the sterile orthodoxy of the exam system.

events.label: The Great Opium Debate
events.description: The Qing court argued between legalizing opium to tax it and stamping it out by force; Daoguang chose suppression and sent Lin Zexu to Canton.

events.label: Lin Zexu Destroys Opium
events.description: Commissioner Lin Zexu confiscated and destroyed over 1,000 tons of British opium at Canton, the principled act that triggered the First Opium War.

events.label: First Opium War
events.description: Britain attacked China to protect the drug trade, and steam-powered gunboats shattered Qing forces, ending China's sense of invulnerability.

events.label: The Nemesis and the Gunboat Gap
events.description: The iron paddle-steamer Nemesis tore through Chinese war junks on inland waters, a technological humiliation that revealed how far China had fallen behind the West.

events.label: Treaty of Nanking
events.description: China's first unequal treaty ceded Hong Kong, opened five treaty ports, imposed a huge indemnity, and began a century of foreign exploitation.

events.label: Hong Kong Ceded to Britain
events.description: The barren island of Hong Kong became a British colony and free port, a foreign foothold on the China coast that would endure for 155 years.

events.label: The Treaty of the Bogue
events.description: A supplementary treaty granted Britain extraterritoriality and most-favored-nation status, so every concession wrung from China now spread automatically to all powers.

events.label: The American and French Treaties
events.description: The United States and France quickly extracted their own unequal treaties at Wanghia and Whampoa, locking China into a multilateral system of foreign privilege.

events.label: The Century of Humiliation Begins
events.description: The defeat and unequal treaties mark the start of what Chinese nationalists call the 'century of humiliation,' a trauma that still shapes modern China.

events.label: Wei Yuan's Maritime Treatise
events.description: The scholar Wei Yuan compiled an 'Illustrated Treatise on the Maritime Kingdoms' urging China to 'learn the superior techniques of the barbarians' — advice ignored for decades.

events.label: Hong Xiuquan's Visions
events.description: A failed exam candidate, after reading Christian tracts, concluded he was the younger brother of Jesus sent to destroy demons and the Manchu regime.

events.label: The God Worshipping Society
events.description: Hong Xiuquan's iconoclastic Christian movement grew among poor Hakka peasants in Guangxi, smashing idols and arming itself for a holy war.

events.label: Taiping Rebellion
events.description: A Christian-inspired peasant revolution built a rebel state across southern China, causing an estimated 20-30 million deaths in history's bloodiest civil war.

events.label: The Heavenly Kingdom at Nanjing
events.description: The Taiping captured Nanjing and made it the 'Heavenly Capital,' ruling a third of China with a theocratic state at war with Confucian civilization itself.

events.label: The Taiping Social Order
events.description: The rebels banned opium, foot-binding, and private land, proclaimed a radical equality of the sexes, and imposed a militarized theocracy on millions.

events.label: The Tianjing Incident
events.description: A murderous power struggle among the Taiping kings massacred tens of thousands of their own and gutted the movement's leadership from within.

events.label: Zeng Guofan and the Xiang Army
events.description: A Confucian scholar-official raised a loyal provincial army in Hunan to fight the Taiping, pioneering the regional militias that saved — and slowly hollowed — the dynasty.

events.label: Li Hongzhang and the Huai Army
events.description: Zeng's brilliant protégé built the Huai Army, helped crush the Taiping, and went on to dominate Qing foreign policy and modernization for a generation.

events.label: The Ever Victorious Army
events.description: A Western-officered mercenary force, finally led by the British officer Charles Gordon, helped the Qing retake the Yangtze delta from the Taiping.

events.label: The Fall of Nanjing
events.description: Zeng Guofan's armies stormed the Heavenly Capital amid mass slaughter, ending the Taiping Rebellion but leaving central China devastated for decades.

events.label: Nian Rebellion
events.description: Mounted bandit armies in the north fought the Qing for nearly two decades, compounding the Taiping catastrophe and further draining the dynasty's resources.

events.label: The Arrow Incident
events.description: British officials used the boarding of a Chinese-owned, Hong Kong-registered ship as a pretext for war, seeking to expand the privileges of 1842.

events.label: Second Opium War
events.description: Anglo-French forces fought their way to Beijing, forcing China to legalize opium, open more ports, and admit foreign diplomats and missionaries.

events.label: Old Summer Palace Destroyed
events.description: British and French troops looted and burned the magnificent Yuanmingyuan, an act of cultural destruction that remains a raw wound in Chinese memory.

events.label: The Treaties of Tianjin and Peking
events.description: New unequal treaties opened ten more ports, legalized opium and the missionary presence, and stationed foreign ministers in Beijing itself.

events.label: Russia Takes the Amur
events.description: Exploiting the war, Russia annexed over 600,000 square miles of Qing territory north of the Amur and along the Pacific, founding Vladivostok on it.

events.label: The Xinyou Coup
events.description: After the Xianfeng Emperor died fleeing the foreign armies, his concubine Cixi joined a palace coup that destroyed the regents and seized power for herself.

events.label: Empress Dowager Cixi
events.description: Cixi dominated Qing politics for nearly five decades, alternately backing and crushing reform as she fought to preserve dynastic — and personal — power.

events.label: Tongzhi Restoration
events.description: Reformist officials stabilized the dynasty after the rebellions, restoring Confucian government and beginning cautious modernization that bought a few more decades.

events.label: The Zongli Yamen
events.description: China grudgingly created its first foreign-affairs office, an admission that the tributary worldview had collapsed and other states had to be dealt with as equals.

events.label: The Muslim Revolts
events.description: Vast revolts among Hui and other Muslims convulsed the northwest and southwest, killing millions and nearly tearing Xinjiang and Yunnan from the empire.

events.label: Reconquering Xinjiang
events.description: The adventurer Yaqub Beg carved out an independent state in Xinjiang until Zuo Zongtang's grueling campaign reconquered it, soon made a regular province.

events.label: Robert Hart and the Maritime Customs
events.description: An Ulsterman ran the foreign-staffed Imperial Maritime Customs Service for decades, providing the Qing its one reliable, honest revenue stream — under foreign control.

events.label: Self-Strengthening Movement
events.description: Qing officials launched arsenals, shipyards, and schools adopting Western military technology while clinging to Confucian values, a half-measure that proved fatally inadequate.

events.label: The Jiangnan Arsenal
events.description: China's largest modern arsenal, near Shanghai, built guns, ships, and a translation bureau, the flagship of the self-strengthening industrial effort.

events.label: The Fuzhou Navy Yard
events.description: A French-built shipyard and naval school at Fuzhou created a modern fleet and a generation of engineers — much of it destroyed by France in a single afternoon in 1884.

events.label: The Tongwen Guan
events.description: Beijing's new school of foreign languages and sciences trained interpreters and engineers, but conservative resistance kept it small and marginal.

events.label: The Chinese Educational Mission
events.description: The Qing sent 120 boys to study in the United States, then recalled them early in conservative panic — among the survivors, China's first modern engineers.

events.label: Steamships, Telegraphs, and Mines
events.description: State-sponsored enterprises like the China Merchants Steam Navigation Company brought modern shipping, telegraphs, and coal mining — profitable but riddled with official graft.

events.label: The Tianjin Massacre
events.description: Rumors that French nuns were killing children sparked a mob that murdered missionaries and diplomats, exposing the explosive anti-Christian tensions of the treaty era.

events.label: The Sino-French War
events.description: France stripped Vietnam from the Qing tributary system and annihilated the Fuzhou fleet, a defeat that exposed the limits of self-strengthening.

events.label: "Chinese Learning as the Base"
events.description: The reforming governor-general Zhang Zhidong built modern industry while coining the slogan that Chinese values must stay the core and Western methods only the tool.

events.label: The Shenbao and a New Public
events.description: Shanghai's commercial newspaper Shenbao created a modern reading public that argued about politics, foreshadowing the opinionated nationalism to come.

events.label: The Diverted Navy Funds
events.description: Money meant for the Beiyang Fleet was siphoned to rebuild Cixi's marble Summer Palace, a symbol of priorities that would be punished at sea in 1894.

events.label: First Sino-Japanese War
events.description: Japan's decisive victory over China shocked the world, exposed Qing military weakness, and cost China control of Korea and Taiwan.

events.label: The Beiyang Fleet Destroyed
events.description: Li Hongzhang's prized modern navy, starved of shells and maintenance, was wrecked at the Yalu and trapped and annihilated at Weihaiwei.

events.label: The Treaty of Shimonoseki
events.description: China ceded Taiwan, recognized Korean independence, and paid a crushing indemnity, the most humiliating treaty yet — and inflicted by an Asian neighbor.

events.label: The Triple Intervention
events.description: Russia, France, and Germany forced Japan to disgorge the Liaodong Peninsula — then collected their own price in concessions, opening the scramble for China.

events.label: The Scramble for Concessions
events.description: After Shimonoseki, the powers seized leaseholds and carved China into spheres of influence, raising real fears the empire would be partitioned like Africa.

events.label: The Open Door Notes
events.description: The United States, lacking a sphere of its own, called for equal commercial access to China — a policy that incidentally helped preserve a nominal Qing unity.

events.label: Kang Youwei and Confucian Reform
events.description: The scholar Kang Youwei reread Confucius as a radical reformer to justify sweeping modernization, organizing protests after the Shimonoseki disaster.

events.label: Liang Qichao and the New Citizen
events.description: Kang's brilliant student used new-style journalism to argue China needed not just new weapons but a transformed, politically active citizenry.

events.label: Hundred Days' Reform
events.description: The Guangxu Emperor launched radical modernization edicts that Cixi crushed after 103 days, imprisoning the emperor and executing the reformers.

events.label: The Coup and the Six Gentlemen
events.description: Cixi's counter-coup ended the reform; six reformers, including Tan Sitong who refused to flee, were beheaded as martyrs of a lost moderate path.

events.label: Yan Fu Translates the West
events.description: Yan Fu's translation of Huxley introduced 'survival of the fittest' to China, framing reform as a desperate struggle against national extinction.

events.label: The Boxers Rise
events.description: Drought and resentment of foreign railways and missionaries fueled the Boxers, peasant militias practicing invulnerability rituals against Christians and 'foreign devils.'

events.label: Boxer Rebellion
events.description: Anti-foreign militants besieged Beijing's legation quarter with Qing court support, provoking an eight-nation invasion and a ruinous indemnity.

events.label: The Siege of the Legations
events.description: For 55 days, foreign diplomats and Chinese Christians held out behind barricades in Beijing while the Qing court vacillated between protecting and attacking them.

events.label: The Eight-Nation Alliance
events.description: An eight-power expeditionary force stormed Beijing, looted the city, and sent the court fleeing to Xi'an in the dynasty's deepest humiliation.

events.label: Mutual Protection of the Southeast
events.description: Powerful southern governors openly ignored the court's declaration of war on the powers, exposing how little authority the dynasty still commanded.

events.label: The Boxer Protocol
events.description: China accepted a 450-million-tael indemnity, foreign troops in the capital, and the execution of officials — the most punitive settlement of the whole era.

events.label: War on Chinese Soil
events.description: Russia and Japan fought the Russo-Japanese War over Manchuria on Chinese territory while a helpless Qing declared neutrality in its own land.

events.label: The New Policies
events.description: After the Boxer catastrophe, Cixi enacted the very reforms she had once crushed — modern schools, a new army, and constitutional preparation — too late to save the dynasty.

events.label: The Abolition of the Examinations
events.description: The Qing scrapped the thirteen-century-old civil service exams overnight, severing the bond between the gentry and the throne and unmooring an entire elite.

events.label: The Constitutional Missions
events.description: Qing commissions toured the world's constitutions and promised a parliament, but a Manchu-dominated 'cabinet' convinced reformers the court would never share power.

events.label: The Deaths of Guangxu and Cixi
events.description: The imprisoned Guangxu Emperor died one day before Cixi — almost certainly poisoned — leaving a hollowed dynasty to a three-year-old child.

events.label: Qiu Jin, the Revolutionary Martyr
events.description: A feminist poet who unbound her feet and trained in Japan, Qiu Jin was executed for plotting an uprising, becoming a heroine of the revolution.

events.label: The Child Emperor Puyi
events.description: The two-year-old Puyi was placed on the throne under his timid father's regency, a fragile child-court facing a gathering revolutionary storm.

events.label: Sun Yat-sen's Revolutionary Alliance
events.description: From exile, Sun Yat-sen united revolutionary groups into the Tongmenghui around his Three Principles of the People — nationalism, democracy, livelihood.

events.label: The Tongmenghui and Its Risings
events.description: Sun's revolutionary alliance launched a string of failed uprisings, including the bloody Huanghuagang revolt, building momentum and martyrs for the cause.

events.label: The Railway Protection Movement
events.description: A court plan to nationalize provincial railways with foreign loans triggered mass protests in Sichuan, the spark that pulled troops away and lit the revolution.

events.label: The Wuchang Uprising
events.description: An accidental bomb blast forced revolutionary soldiers in Wuchang to rise early on 10 October 1911; within weeks most provinces declared independence.

events.label: Xinhai Revolution
events.description: Republican revolutionaries overthrew the Qing dynasty, ending over two thousand years of imperial rule in China.

events.label: Yuan Shikai's Bargain
events.description: Recalled to save the dynasty with his Beiyang Army, Yuan Shikai instead brokered the emperor's abdication in exchange for the presidency of the new republic.

events.label: The Republic of China Proclaimed
events.description: Revolutionaries declared the Republic of China at Nanjing with Sun Yat-sen as provisional president, the first republic in Asia.

events.label: Puyi Abdicates
events.description: The six-year-old last emperor abdicated on 12 February 1912, ending the Qing dynasty and two millennia of Chinese imperial rule.

events.label: The Articles of Favourable Treatment
events.description: The abdication deal let Puyi keep his title, a pension, and the Forbidden City — a ghost court that lingered until his expulsion in 1924.

events.label: The Qing Inheritance
events.description: The republic inherited the Qing's vast multiethnic borders and its 'century of humiliation' grievance — the territorial and emotional foundations of modern China.
